This week its designated Supervisor for these accounts, Rosalind Beaufort, faces four files. File 1: an equities trader saw on the firm's order-management system that a discretionary account will buy a thinly traded stock this afternoon, and bought the stock for his personal account first. File 2: Registered Representative Quentin Verrick, who conducts business on behalf of Dunhaven & Achebe, has been asked by a long-time client to exercise discretion over a discretionary account the client keeps at a different Investment Dealer. File 3: two managed-account proposals arrive, each with the client's prior written consent on file — one to buy shares of an issuer connected to the Dealer Member, the other to sell bonds from a managed account directly to the personal account of an Associate Portfolio Manager. File 4: a client of an ordinary advisory account places an order to sell exactly 8,000 shares of one issuer and asks her Registered Representative to choose the moment during the day to execute at the best available price.
questions.stem: Applying section 3280 to the two managed-account proposals in File 3, what should Rosalind conclude?

options.A: Both proposals may proceed because written client consent cures any managed-account conflict
options.B: Both proposals are prohibited regardless of consent
options.C: The connected-issuer purchase may proceed with the prior written consent, but the sale to the Associate Portfolio Manager's account is prohibited because subsection 3280(3) contains no consent exception
options.D: The sale to the Associate Portfolio Manager's account may proceed with consent, but the connected-issuer purchase requires Corporation approval

questions.rationale: Subsection 3280(2) prohibits knowingly allowing a managed account to invest in a security of an issuer related or connected to the Dealer Member 'without the prior written consent of the client' — so consent permits the first proposal. Subsection 3280(3), by contrast, flatly prohibits knowingly causing a managed account to purchase or sell a security from or to the account of a Portfolio Manager, an Associate Portfolio Manager or their associates, with no consent exception — so the second proposal is blocked. Option A is the closest distractor: it correctly handles the first proposal but wrongly extends the consent cure to the absolute 3280(3) prohibitions. Options B and D each misclassify one proposal.
questions.source_quote: "A responsible person or a Dealer Member must not knowingly cause any managed account to: (i) purchase or sell a security or derivative of a security of an issuer from or to the account of a Portfolio Manager, an Associate Portfolio Manager" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5463

questions.scenario: Dunhaven & Achebe Wealth, an Investment Dealer, operates both a discretionary account program and a managed account program. This week its designated Supervisor for these accounts, Rosalind Beaufort, faces four files. File 1: an equities trader saw on the firm's order-management system that a discretionary account will buy a thinly traded stock this afternoon, and bought the stock for his personal account first. File 2: Registered Representative Quentin Verrick, who conducts business on behalf of Dunhaven & Achebe, has been asked by a long-time client to exercise discretion over a discretionary account the client keeps at a different Investment Dealer. File 3: two managed-account proposals arrive, each with the client's prior written consent on file — one to buy shares of an issuer connected to the Dealer Member, the other to sell bonds from a managed account directly to the personal account of an Associate Portfolio Manager. File 4: a client of an ordinary advisory account places an order to sell exactly 8,000 shares of one issuer and asks her Registered Representative to choose the moment during the day to execute at the best available price.
questions.stem: In File 4, the account is an ordinary advisory account with no discretionary account agreement. Applying section 3221, may the Registered Representative choose the moment to execute the client's 8,000-share sell order?

options.A: No — any exercise of discretion requires a discretionary account agreement
options.B: Yes — subsection 3221(2) provides that the prohibition does not apply to time and price discretion exercised in fulfilling the best execution obligation relating to a client order for a specific amount or a specific security
options.C: Yes — Registered Representatives may exercise unlimited discretion in advisory accounts
options.D: No — only a Portfolio Manager may select execution timing

questions.rationale: Subsection 3221(2) provides that the prohibition in subsection 3221(1) does not apply to time and price discretion exercised in fulfilling the Dealer Member's best execution obligation relating to a client order for a specific amount or a specific security — here the client specified both the security and the amount, leaving only time and price to the Representative. Option A is the closest distractor because subsection 3221(1) does prohibit discretionary trading, 'including time and price discretion', outside Part G accounts — but these facts fall squarely within the 3221(2) exception. Options C and D respectively overstate and understate who may act.
questions.source_quote: "Subsection 3221(1) does not apply to time and price discretion exercised in fulfilling the Dealer Member's best execution obligation relating to a client order for a specific amount or a specific security." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4921
